transcript: Welcome back everyone. Today we're going to continue our discussion on search algorithms. Last class we covered breadth-first search and depth-first search, and today we're going to look at informed search strategies. The key idea is that we can do better than blind search if we have some additional knowledge about the problem. The first algorithm I want to talk about is A-star search. A-star search uses a heuristic function h of n that estimates the cost from node n to the goal. The evaluation function f of n equals g of n plus h of n, where g of n is the actual cost from the start to n, and h of n is the estimated cost from n to the goal. What makes A-star special is that it's both complete and optimal under the right conditions. A-star is optimal if the heuristic is admissible, meaning it never overestimates the true cost to reach the goal. Let me give you a concrete example. Consider a grid world where you want to get from point A to point B. You can move up, down, left, or right. The Manhattan distance — that's the sum of horizontal and vertical distances — is an admissible heuristic because it never overestimates the true shortest path distance. You can't take a shortcut through walls. Now there's another important property I want you to know for the midterm, and that's consistency. A heuristic is consistent, also called monotone, if for every node n and every successor n-prime generated by any action a, the heuristic at n is less than or equal to the step cost of getting to n-prime plus the heuristic at n-prime. In math notation: h of n is less than or equal to c of n, a, n-prime plus h of n-prime. Why does consistency matter? Because consistency implies admissibility, but not the other way around. And with a consistent heuristic, A-star never needs to re-expand nodes, which makes it much more efficient in practice. Let's compare this to what we learned last week. Breadth-first search explores all nodes at depth d before moving to depth d plus 1. It's complete and optimal for unit-cost steps, but its space complexity is exponential — O of b to the d. Depth-first search uses much less memory, O of b times m, but it's not complete in infinite spaces and not optimal. A-star with a good heuristic can dramatically reduce the number of nodes we need to expand. In our grid world example, BFS might explore thousands of nodes, while A-star with Manhattan distance might only need to explore a few dozen. For the midterm, you'll need to know how to prove whether a given heuristic is admissible, how to prove consistency, the time and space complexity of BFS, DFS, and A-star, and how to trace through A-star on a small graph showing the open and closed lists at each step. Any questions before we move on to the next topic?
